In this lesson, we're going to talk about how to make decisions based on your main marketing metrics. And we're going to discuss six simple common scenarios. The first scenario we're going to talk about is what if your cost per sale equals your average front end value. The second scenario is what if my cost per click is higher than my earnings per click. The third scenario we're going to talk about is what if I have a campaign with a really high cost per click. The next scenario is what if I have two advertising campaigns and one has a higher cost per click. Should I cancel it? The next scenario is what if my cost per sale is higher than my average front end value. The final scenario that we're going to go through is what if my lifetime customer value is not increasing. What if my cost per sale equals my average front end value? What that means is that you're not generating any profit when acquiring a new customer, nor are you losing any money when you acquire a new customer. That is perfectly fine as you've heard me say throughout this training. The reality is that on the front, on the front end, when acquiring a new customer, your goal is not to generate a profit. Your goal is not to have your average front end value exceed your cost per sale. Your goal is to invest as much money as you possibly can into customer acquisition. You acquire maximum new customers and right now at break even. What you want to do is any campaign where your cost per sale equals your average front end value, you want to continue running that particular campaign. The next scenario, what if my cost per click is higher than my earnings per click? That means that you're paying more to generate a click to generate a visitor than what that click or visitor is worth to you. In this particular campaign, the more clicks that you get on this campaign, the more money you're losing on the front end. What you need to do is you need to either, one, optimize the ad campaign that you're using to decrease your click costs. First is optimize whatever marketing medium, whatever advertising channel you're using to decrease your click costs. Or two, you need to increase your overall sales conversion rate, meaning you need to increase the percentage of prospects that you're turning into customers. And or three, you need to increase the average front end transaction value. What those three things will do is help you bring up your earnings per click and or decrease your cost per click. The next scenario is what if I have a campaign with a really high cost per click? What should I do? Maybe it's really high compared to all of the other campaigns that you have. Well, again, a high cost per click is all relative. There really is no such thing as a high cost per click. You need to look at cost per click along with earnings per click to determine how high you can go. High is only relative to your EPC. The higher your EPC is, the higher your cost per click can be. This is why as a side note that when you have an optimized funnel, like what you're learning with the six-figure funnel formula, and you're able to have significantly higher earnings per click than your competitors, what that really means is that you can far outspend them, meaning you can spend more, a lot more. You can afford to pay some of the much higher per click costs that they can't because they don't have the EPC that you do. Therefore, in the long run, you will ultimately be able to crush them. For example, you could have a cost per click getting back to this scenario. You could have a cost per click of $15, but if your earnings per click are $15, then that's okay. You're at break even, and that's totally cool. Next scenario, what if I have two advertising campaigns and one has a higher CPC, a much higher CPC than the other one? Should you cancel it? Well, higher cost per click, again, is not necessarily a bad thing. If both of those campaigns are allowing you to break even, do not cancel either one. In other words, no matter how high one campaign is compared to another, if that campaign is still allowing you to acquire new customers at no cost to you, continue to run it. You may make the other one a priority and funnel more money into the one with the lower cost per click, but the reality is any campaign that allows you to acquire new customers at break even is a good thing. Remember, it's all about return on investment. It's not about cost per click. It's not about opt-in rate. It's not about sales conversion rate. At the end of the day, the only thing that you can deposit is return on investment. What that means is that in this context is that it doesn't matter how high the cost per click is. It allows you to acquire a new customer at break even and you have a back end, meaning that you know that you're going to extract more and more profit from that customer. Well, you just acquired a valuable and growing asset for free, no matter how high the cost per click is. Again, always keep your cost per click within the right context. It always needs to be viewed along with earnings per click to mean anything. Next, what if my cost per sale is higher than my average front end value? That means, of course, that you're paying more to generate a new customer than what that customer actually spends with you on their first transaction. You need to do either one, again, optimize the ad campaign to decrease your costs. By decreasing your ad costs, it'll help to bring down your cost per sale. Two, you need to increase your overall sales conversion rate, meaning you need to convert more prospects into customers. Three, you need to increase your average front end transaction value. Again, I already mentioned earlier that once your business matures and you have the cash flow, going negative on the front end is what's going to help you grow your business faster. Again, assuming that you have a nice back end. As your business matures and as your business grows and as you have more and more and greater cash flow, having a cost per sale on the front end that's higher than the average front end transaction value is okay. In fact, going negative is what most of the biggest direct response companies do because they know that going negative allows them to acquire more customers than if they only went to break even. Finally, what if my lifetime customer value is not increasing? Well, if your lifetime customer value is not increasing, that means that you're not growing the average value of a customer over the lifespan of their patronage in your business. Now, if your lifetime customer value is not increasing, this is an indication that you're not making enough new offers frequently enough to existing customers. You need to be and become more prolific in this case with your back end product and offer creation. We're going to be talking about this, the back end and being prolific and offer creation in module 12. For now, what I want you to understand is that if the average customer, if the value of an average customer is not growing, that means you do not have an effective back end. You know that the real business, the business we're in, is the back end. That's an indication that you need to work more on the back end. Again, you'll learn all about that in module 12.
